title: BUG: DataFrame.groupby() raises KeyError on missing MultiIndex column
body: Reproducer: a DataFrame with a MultiIndex header. Calling df.groupby('missing_col') used to raise KeyError with a helpful message; in 2.2.0 it now raises KeyError: 0 from internal _get_label_or_level_values, which is much harder to diagnose.

title: Series.dtype is wrong after concat with all-NaT entries
body: After pd.concat([s1, s2]) where s2 is entirely NaT, the resulting dtype becomes object instead of datetime64[ns]. Reproducer attached. Regression from 2.1.x where the dtype was preserved.

title: BUG: pd.read_csv chunksize iterator returns extra empty chunk at EOF
body: Calling list(pd.read_csv(file, chunksize=1000)) returns N + 1 elements where the last one is an empty DataFrame. Should be exactly N. Affects any code that counts chunks.

title: to_parquet fails for object columns with mixed-type NaN handling
body: df.to_parquet(path) raises pyarrow.ArrowTypeError: Could not convert NaN with type float to object when an object column contains both strings and NaN. Worked in 2.0.x.

title: Regression: DataFrame.merge how='outer' silently drops keys with NaN
body: Merging two frames where one key column has NaN values used to keep NaN groups; on 2.1.0 those rows are dropped from the result. No deprecation warning was issued.

title: pandas.DataFrame.merge API docs do not list the 'validate' parameter
body: The validate kwarg has existed since 0.24 but the public api docs page for DataFrame.merge omits it. The values one_to_one, one_to_many, many_to_one, many_to_many should be documented.

title: Typo in user_guide/10min.rst — 'colums' should be 'columns'
body: Section 'Selection' has 'access multiple colums by name' — should be 'columns'. Tiny doc fix.

title: Broken link in API reference for pd.read_sql
body: The 'See also' block on the read_sql page links to a 404 (SQLAlchemy tutorial that has moved). Link target needs updating to the new URL.

title: Missing example for Series.between_time
body: The docstring for Series.between_time has parameter descriptions but no usage example. Most other time-window methods have one — please add a small example with a DatetimeIndex.

title: Docs claim Series.unique returns sorted values; it returns insertion order
body: The user guide section on duplicates says 'unique() returns sorted unique values' but the actual behavior returns the order of first occurrence. The docs sentence should be corrected.

title: ENH: support timezone-aware datetime indices in Series.between_time
body: Currently Series.between_time raises if the index is tz-aware. It would be useful to support tz-aware indices and let the start/end times be interpreted in that timezone.

title: Allow numpy.float128 dtype in DataFrame.astype on platforms where it exists
body: On Linux x86_64 numpy supports float128. Asking DataFrame.astype('float128') falls back to object. Would be nice to honor it where supported and document the platform caveat.

title: Performance: bring back the cython fast path in groupby().agg() for sum/mean
body: After the 2.1 refactor groupby.agg('sum') went from C-implemented to Python-dispatched and is ~3x slower on the wide-DataFrame benchmark. Restoring the cython path (or adding a pure-numpy short-circuit) would close the gap.

title: Allow callable as `keys` argument in DataFrame.set_index
body: set_index accepts a column name, list of columns, or array; it does not accept a callable that receives the DataFrame and returns a key. df.groupby() supports callable keys for symmetry; set_index should too.

title: API DESIGN: deprecate inplace= kwarg across all methods
body: inplace=True is unsafe (it sometimes returns None, sometimes a copy) and discourages method chaining. Proposing a single deprecation cycle that removes it from all methods. Would make the API more consistent.

title: How do I install pandas with the optional excel/parquet/sql dependencies?
body: I see references to extras like pandas[parquet] but the install docs don't mention them by name. Is there a canonical list of extras and what they pull in?

title: What's the recommended way to process a 50GB CSV that doesn't fit in memory?
body: Chunked read_csv works but feels manual. Is dask recommended? Is there a way to do groupby + agg over chunks idiomatically with pandas alone?

title: Why does df.loc[idx] sometimes return a Series and sometimes a DataFrame?
body: If idx is a scalar I get a Series; if it's a list of one element I get a 1-row DataFrame. Is this documented somewhere? It bites me every time.

title: Best practice for time series resampling with irregularly-sampled data?
body: I have a DateTimeIndex with very irregular gaps (some 1s apart, some hours). resample('1min').mean() works but feels wrong because most bins are empty. Is there an idiomatic pandas pattern for this kind of data?

title: How to convert a nested-dict-of-dicts to a DataFrame efficiently?
body: I have data like {a: {x:1, y:2}, b: {x:3, y:4}}. DataFrame.from_dict works but I'm not sure which `orient=` option is fastest at scale. Are there benchmarks?

title: Series indexing with boolean masks of different length silently broadcasts
body: If I have a Series of length 5 and pass a boolean mask of length 3, pandas 2.2 broadcasts the mask to length 5 (repeating) instead of raising. I'd expect an IndexingError. Ambiguity: could be 'feature' if it's an intentional convenience, but the silent broadcast is surprising and produces wrong results.

title: pandas.DataFrame.merge docs don't explain the validate parameter — what does 'one_to_one' do?
body: I'd like the docs to explain each validate option with an example. Ambiguity: this is partly a question (I want to know how it works) and partly a docs ask (I want it documented). The fix is a docs PR.

title: BUG + DOC: read_csv silently strips a UTF-8 BOM, and the docs don't mention this
body: Reading a UTF-8 CSV with BOM, the first column header is missing the BOM (good) but pandas does this silently — no warning, and no mention in the docs of read_csv. Both a bug-fix candidate (warn) and a docs gap. Ambiguity: multi-class overlap; primary fix is the silent strip.

title: Wouldn't it be nice if DataFrame.to_dict had an option to flatten MultiIndex?
body: Right now to_dict on a MultiIndex DataFrame produces tuple keys, which are hard to JSON-serialize. A flatten='/' option would join the levels. Ambiguity: this is phrased as a question/wish but is fundamentally a feature request.

title: Docs say df.copy() is shallow by default but the implementation is deep
body: user_guide/copy_on_write.rst claims DataFrame.copy() does a shallow copy unless deep=True is passed; in fact 2.2's implementation deep-copies by default. Ambiguity: this could be filed as a bug in the code, but the consensus is the docs are wrong and the deep default is intended.
